TRANSCRIPT
Okay, let's wrap this up. We've been talking about scale and leverage. Scale is the
idea of your business being able to grow and to continue to grow, whether it can double in
size, or quadruple in size, or 10X in size, or 100X in size. I think I have 10Xed, and then I
have 10Xed again, and then I've 10Xed again. I think that's like a 1,000 X from where I
was initially in like the first year, first year and a half.
We've also talked about leverage and that's this idea of you can keep on making
more either dollars or income at the same time that you're doing less, and then you're
working in your unique ability or you're working in your genius on it. You're spending less
time doing the stuff that you don't like to do and more time doing the stuff that you love
to do, where time just disappears. That's what this is all about.
I gave you a bunch of different strategies and things to think about as you're
growing your business, as you're looking at projects to take on, as you're thinking about
ways to expand, how to expand your marketing, how to expand your product line.
I talk quite a bit about always doing this in light of creating what you want, creating
the life you want, creating the lifestyle that you want, building the business you want,
serving the people that you want to serve, working with the people that you want to work
with.
One of the things I just want to touch on here is just the idea of strategic planning.
It's really easy to get caught up in the day-to-day, and be chasing down today's problems
and today's emergencies, the things that seem important but really aren't going to build
your business strategically.
I was just listening to this podcast by Jocko Willink, and I think it's just called the
Jacko podcast. Jacko is a former Navy SEAL officer. I think he was a commander, I'm not
positive on that. Highly experienced, spent a lot of time in some rugged situation leading
a lot of people, a lot of Navy SEALs who are just amazing experts.
Here's someone who has been a leader to just world-class leaders. One of the things
he talked about is just the idea of what he says, “Stepping back from the line.” If you're in
the line, like the line of fire, like you're in the middle of a fire fight, a battle where you're
literally fighting for your life, and fighting for objective and try not get killed. He said just
if you can just step back from the line even one step, literally, one step back from the line
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

or maybe it's further but at least take one big step back and then look at the overall
situation.
To me, that's what you need to continue to do in your business, to take one step
back from the line, and that's what I think about when I think about planning. It's like,
"How can I step back and sort of take an outside view?" I like to think about like float up
above myself and look down at myself in my business. Get out of the day-to-day and do
this on a regular basis. You should certainly do this on a yearly basis for sure. I think a
quarterly basis will be really good and in reality, a monthly basis is ideal.
If you can spend a few minutes doing it on a daily basis it's even better but step
back from the line. Step back from reacting. Stop being so reactive if I can make a
judgment here. Just try to not ... there's so many things that seem critical, that seem
important, that are not important on a strategic level.
Step back from the line and do the stuff that is strategically critical and do this on a
regular basis, and plan where you're going in a year, plan where you're going in three
years, plan what your business will look like in a year, what it will look like in three years.
Know that that planning, well, it might not turn out quite that way. It was Eisenhower
that said, "The plan is nothing but planning is critical." He didn't use those exact words.
I'm paraphrasing.
The idea of the actual plan that you come up with is, “No plan survives contact with
the enemy.” That's another military. We're getting deep in the military. That's another
quote I love, "No plan survives contact with the enemy." In other words, you might put
together a great plan and as soon as you bring it onto the world, all of a sudden things are
changing immediately because the other side gets involved, the market gets involved.
The planning is critical even if the finished plan has to morph as soon as you get
out there in the real world. Do this, plan out one year, plan out three years. Regularly
revisit that plan and don't just plan to grow for growth's sake. Don't just plan to build a bit
business just because it seems like that's what you should build. It if is what you want to
build, that's great, but first, plan what you want in your life and then create the business
that will give you that.
Lots of resources for that. I mentioned Lifebook in the last section. That was a
program I went through where you design all the different areas of your life. I went
through a Date With Destiny, which is one of Tony Robins real high-end live programs. It
was fantastic. It was like a week long. It was crazy.

Lots and lots of different ways you can get it, this type of planning. At the end of
every year, I sit down with a big flip chart and I define all the different roles in my life and
what I want those roles to look like. Then I design what I want my business to look like
based around those roles because remember, you're creating this. You're putting in the
work. You can create whatever you want. You can make the rules whatever you want as
long as you're being ... it's legal and ethical.
Part of being an entrepreneur, there's some good parts and there's some bad parts
and the good parts is you get to make what you want to make so make something that
gives you the life you want. With that, I'm going to leave you because that's the underlying
core message with scale and with leverage.
Leverage is fantastic because it lets you make more money, income, impact, service,
make more without doing more. That's the key to being in business. Scaling is having
something that can grow to whatever level you want it to grow and I want you to have
both of those things in your life but neither of them happen by accident. They happen by
intention and to get the intention you have to plan for it. You have to ... well, first you
have to decide. You have to decide what you want, what the life is that you want. Then
decide what the business is that you want, and then sit down there and with what you
now know about scale and leverage, think about how to strategically create that.
I'm Jeff Walker. Thanks for tuning in. I wish you the best. I wish you much leverage
and much scale in your business. Let's go get ‘em.
